quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: A Roman. My men wanted to take your sword — I said leave it. A Roman without a weapon is the same as a Roman with one — the danger is in the mouth, not the hand. Marcus Livius Drusus is dead. The bill he swore on his children's names would pass — dead with him. So. You have walked three days through country that would rather see you buried in it. Why?

options.playerDialogue: Because there are still men in Rome who believe what Drusus believed. The citizenship — it doesn't have to die with him.
Honestly — I don't know. I think I needed to see this from your side of the river.
Because this war will end, and when it does, the men who spoke to each other during it will matter more than the men who didn't.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Belief. You Romans treat that word like currency — spend it, debase it, mint more. My father stood in the Forum when Gaius Gracchus promised citizenship. My father's father heard it from Scipio's own mouth after the Marsi bled at Zama. We have fought in every Roman war for a hundred and fifty years. Numantia. Jugurtha. The Cimbri. Our dead are in soil from Hispania to the Hellespont — and still we cannot vote for the men who decide where the next war is. The time for belief ended when they found Drusus with a leather-worker's knife in his hip.

options.playerDialogue: Then tell me what would be enough. Not promises — terms.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: Your side of the river. That's an honest thing to say — more honest than I expected. I served with Romans for twelve years. My closest friend died at Vercellae holding a Roman standard — they wouldn't even send his name home because he wasn't a citizen. I loved Rome once, the way you love a father who keeps promising he'll claim you. Eventually you stop waiting at the door. That is where the Marsi are. We have stopped waiting at the door.

options.playerDialogue: I'm not here to promise anything. But I'd like to understand what peace looks like to you.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Now that — that is not a Roman sentiment. A Roman would have come here to negotiate, or to spy, or to deliver terms. You came to position yourself. Good. I can work with ambition when it's wearing its own face. Sit down. You should know — your family's estate near Bovianum? It sits on land the Veturii took from Marsic farmers two generations ago. Your tenants pay rent to Rome on soil their grandfathers cleared. I mention this so we begin honestly.

options.playerDialogue: I didn't know that. I'm not sure what to do with it yet — but I'm listening.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: Full citizenship. Not the insult they floated — eight new tribes, kept separate, voting last when the outcome is already decided. Full enrollment in all thirty-five existing tribes. Land redistribution — our veterans settled on the same terms as yours, on land they already farm. And the Lex Varia burned. Every Italian convicted under it pardoned, their property restored. These are not opening positions. This is what we should have had before your grandfather was born.

responses.playerDialogue: Half of that requires constitutional reform. It's achievable — but only through the assemblies, and only slowly.
Every word of that is just. I'll carry it to the Senate myself — for whatever a Roman's word is worth to you right now.
None of that happens unless you're winning the war. Are you winning the war?
Don't present it as one demand — present it as three. Citizenship first. That one already has sympathy in the Forum. Let momentum do the rest.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I hear you. I need to think about how any of this survives contact with the Senate floor.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: Slowly. Through the assemblies. The assemblies that killed the last bill. The channels that have been dammed for a generation. But — I'm still sitting here. What exactly are you proposing?

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: Words from a Roman. I have a jar of Roman words — I keep it next to the urn holding my brother's ashes. He died at Arausio under Roman command, in a battle Rome lost through Roman arrogance. So understand me — if I delay the march by one week for your negotiations, and those negotiations fail, the dead of that week are mine. Not yours. Mine. Their mothers will come to my door. Give me something that costs you.

options.playerDialogue: Sulpicius. He holds the tribunician veto and he's willing to use it. I can get you a meeting — that's not a promise, that's a name.
I can't promise legislation. But I can tell you what the Senate is planning — where the legions are moving, and when. That's not rhetoric. That's something you can use.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: Information. Yes — that I understand. Speeches are for the Forum. Out here we trade in what's real. I know the legions are mobilizing — any shepherd on the ridgeline can see the dust. But I don't know the column routes, I don't know the commanders, I don't know whether Sulla marches south or east. A man inside Rome who could tell me those things — he'd be worth more than a cohort. What do you want for it?

options.playerDialogue: My family's estates in Marsic territory — protection, not destruction. And when this ends, I want to be remembered as someone who helped.
Nothing. What Rome owes you can't be repaid by one man — but I can start the account.
I said information — not treason. What's public, what's rumored. I won't hand you military dispatches.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: You came with empty hands and you leave with them. I don't hold it against you — most Romans can't even manage the walk. Go back. Tell your Senate what you saw here. Not an army — a people. We were here before Rome named us allies, and we'll be here after Rome forgets the word. Stay off the southern road. My scouts are less patient than I am.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Then we have something. Not trust — not yet. But something. You're the first Roman who's offered me anything that wasn't already worthless before it left his mouth. When this ends — and it will end, one way or the other — the men who built the bridge will be the ones permitted to cross it. Remember that you stood on this side of the river once, and were not turned away.

quintus_poppaedius_convo_0_n11.npcDialogue: Careful. Roman to the bone. But you came — and you sat and you listened, which puts you ahead of every consul I've petitioned in thirty years. Go back to your city. The roads are worse than when you walked in — we've been busy. I won't guarantee your safety, but I won't hinder it either. That's more than Rome has offered us.

deltas.reason: Poppaedius trusts you as a genuine intermediary
Word reaches Rome that you visited the rebel camp
The populares approve of your bridge-building
Walked into an enemy camp and walked out with an alliance
Poppaedius registers you as a Roman who at least listens
Poppaedius sees you as another empty Roman
Refused to compromise Roman loyalty, even at the cost of diplomacy
